summary: Gruber responds to Tim Bray's criticism of iCal after Bray lost his calendar data to file corruption following a system lockup. While agreeing the data loss is unacceptable, Gruber methodically investigates Bray's specific technical claims and finds some of them off-base — iCal's use of UUIDs for folder names is defensible, and its file-saving behavior already appears to use atomic writes rather than in-place updates. However, Gruber agrees with Bray's core argument: iCal should keep backup revisions of calendar data so that corruption doesn't mean total loss. The real failure isn't the corruption itself but iCal's lack of recovery capabilities.
key_points: iCal's use of UUIDs for calendar folder names is a reasonable design choice that prevents name collisions and survives renames, not the design flaw Bray suggests
Investigation using hfsdebug shows iCal already uses atomic saves (writing to new files rather than updating in-place), contrary to Bray's assumption
The real problem is iCal's failure to keep backup revisions of calendar data files, which would make corruption recoverable
Calendar data is high-value and small — there's no excuse for not maintaining at least one backup revision for disaster recovery
Most users wouldn't know where to find calendar data files or how to recover them, making iCal's lack of built-in recovery especially harmful
money_quotes: Bray's basic criticism as to why this shouldn't be possible is sound. But I don't think he has it quite right.
These iCal data file folders aren't meant to be end-user-serviceable files; you're meant to interact with them solely through iCal, not the Finder.
Bray's fourth point is the crux of the matter, however. There's no reason iCal couldn't keep the previous file around.
Losing an entire calendar is potentially devastating; losing the changes from your current iCal session would be, in most cases, merely annoying.
Disk space is cheap, calendar files are small, and calendar data is, in many cases, very important.
The problem is its meager data corruption recovery capabilities.
Wise users take this into their own hands, and back up all of their important data on their own, every day. But most people don't back up regularly.
key_insight: The real failure in iCal's design isn't that data corruption can happen, but that it offers no recovery path when it does — keeping backup revisions of small, high-value calendar files is an obvious safeguard that's inexcusably absent.
